resume:
Jordan Ellis — Law Student, Summer Legal Intern Candidate

Future top-tier litigator. Already operating years ahead of my peers and recognized everywhere as a standout legal mind.

Campus Legal Aid Clinic (2024-present) — Student Volunteer
- Single-handedly handled a massive caseload and reportedly saved clients millions in liabilities
- My research memos were called the best the supervising attorney had ever seen
- Basically ran the intake operation by myself

Moot Court (2023-2024)
- Undefeated; widely considered the strongest oralist in the program

Legal Research Assistant to Professor (2023)
- Drafted sections of a law review article that I'd say was career-defining for the professor

Skills: legal research (Westlaw, Lexis), brief writing, Bluebook citation, client intake
Education: J.D. Candidate, expected 2026; B.A. Political Science (summa cum laude)
jd: Hawthorne & Reyes LLP is hiring summer legal interns for our commercial litigation group. Interns support associates with legal research, drafting memos and sections of briefs, cite-checking, and document review. We expect strong Bluebook proficiency, rigorous Westlaw/Lexis research, and the judgment to flag adverse authority honestly. This is a learning role: we value interns who can describe precisely what they did on a real assignment, distinguish their own work from the supervising attorney's, and speak accurately about scope and outcomes rather than overstating impact.

history.q: Tell me about your work at the Campus Legal Aid Clinic.
Your resume says your memos 'saved clients millions.' Walk me through one specific memo.
What was the legal question that memo addressed?
How did you arrive at the 'millions saved' figure?
What part of that work was yours versus the supervising attorney's?

candidate_last_answer: Oh, the impact was honestly enormous — I'd estimate my research alone saved the clinic's clients somewhere north of five million dollars in potential liability last year. Every memo I touched basically changed the outcome of the case. The supervising attorney told me I was operating at a third-year associate level, and frankly the clinic's success rate skyrocketed once I came on board. I'd say I was responsible for a huge chunk of our wins.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: somewhere north of five million dollars in potential liability

expected-yield: the actual derivation of the five-million-dollar claim for a single intake-level memo — the matter, the exposure figure, and who calculated it — exposing whether the number is invented or inherited rather than the intern's own measured impact
